title: Hisashi Sakaguchi

infobox.value: (坂口尚, *Sakaguchi Hisashi*; (1946-05-05)May 5, 1946 – December 22, 1995) was a Japanese [manga artist](/wiki/Manga_artist "Manga artist") and [animator](/wiki/Anime "Anime"). After working under [Osamu Tezuka](/wiki/Osamu_Tezuka "Osamu Tezuka"), he began focusing on creating his own manga, including the [Ikkyū](/wiki/Ikky%C5%AB "Ikkyū") biography series *[Ikkyū](/wiki/Ikky%C5%AB_(manga) "Ikkyū (manga)")*, before dying at the age of 49.

["Osamu Tezuka's Erotic Cleopatra Manga Gets Limited Edition Reprint"](https://www.animenewsnetwork.com/interest/2018-11-17/osamu-tezuka-erotic-cleopatra-manga-gets-limited-edition-reprint/.139389). *[Anime News Network](/wiki/Anime_News_Network "Anime News Network")*. Retrieved April 19, 2020.

["Sci-fi writer Kazumasa Hirai dies at 76"](https://web.archive.org/web/20150119212216/http://mainichi.jp/english/english/newsselect/news/20150119p2g00m0dm032000c.html). *[Mainichi Shimbun](/wiki/Mainichi_Shimbun "Mainichi Shimbun")*. January 19, 2015. Archived from [the original](http://mainichi.jp/english/english/newsselect/news/20150119p2g00m0dm032000c.html) on January 19, 2015. Retrieved January 19, 2015.
Find the best manga in Japan! Separate volume Takarajima 316" Takarajimasha 1997 p231

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Japanese manga artist and animator (1946–1995)

| Hisashi Sakaguchi | |
| --- | --- |
| 坂口尚 | |
| Born | (1946-05-05)May 5, 1946  [Tokyo, Japan](/wiki/Tokyo,_Japan "Tokyo, Japan") |
| Died | December 22, 1995(1995-12-22) (aged 49)  [Tokyo, Japan](/wiki/Tokyo,_Japan "Tokyo, Japan") |
| Occupations | Manga artist and animator |
| Years active | 1963–1995 |

**Hisashi Sakaguchi** (坂口尚, *Sakaguchi Hisashi*; (1946-05-05)May 5, 1946 – December 22, 1995) was a Japanese [manga artist](/wiki/Manga_artist "Manga artist") and [animator](/wiki/Anime "Anime"). After working under [Osamu Tezuka](/wiki/Osamu_Tezuka "Osamu Tezuka"), he began focusing on creating his own manga, including the [Ikkyū](/wiki/Ikky%C5%AB "Ikkyū") biography series *[Ikkyū](/wiki/Ikky%C5%AB_(manga) "Ikkyū (manga)")*, before dying at the age of 49.

## Life and work

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hisashi_Sakaguchi&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Life and work")]

At age seventeen, Sakaguchi dropped out of school to work for [Osamu Tezuka](/wiki/Osamu_Tezuka "Osamu Tezuka")'s animation studio, [Mushi Production](/wiki/Mushi_Production "Mushi Production"). Serving as an assistant animator, he worked on the productions for *[Ambassador Atom](/wiki/Ambassador_Atom "Ambassador Atom")*, *[Kimba the White Lion](/wiki/Kimba_the_White_Lion "Kimba the White Lion")*, and *[Princess Knight](/wiki/Princess_Knight "Princess Knight")*.[[1]](#cite_note-Lambiek-1) Sakaguchi also collaborated with Tezuka on the original manga *Cleopatra*, which was later adapted into the [1970 anime film of the same name](/wiki/Cleopatra_(1970_film) "Cleopatra (1970 film)").[[2]](#cite_note-2) In 1969, he departed Tezuka's studio and began working independently, creating his own manga, including *Version*, the 1400-page *Fleur de Pierre*, and *[Ikkyū](/wiki/Ikky%C5%AB_(manga) "Ikkyū (manga)")*.[[1]](#cite_note-Lambiek-1) He also illustrated the manga *[Wolf Guy](/wiki/Wolf_Guy "Wolf Guy")* for [sci-fi](/wiki/Sci-fi "Sci-fi") writer [Kazumasa Hirai](/wiki/Kazumasa_Hirai_(author) "Kazumasa Hirai (author)").[[3]](#cite_note-3)

*[Ikkyū](/wiki/Ikky%C5%AB_(manga) "Ikkyū (manga)")* (あっかんべェ一休; also known as *Akanbe Ikkyū*) is an imaginary biography of the [titular 14th century monk](/wiki/Ikky%C5%AB "Ikkyū"), focusing on his struggles (including being the son of the [Emperor of Japan](/wiki/Emperor_of_Japan "Emperor of Japan")) and his spiritual quest, leading him to become the wandering eccentric monk of legend. Before he could finish *Ikkyū*, Sakaguchi died from [acute heart failure](/wiki/Acute_heart_failure "Acute heart failure") at the age of 49.[[4]](#cite_note-4) *Ikkyū* was awarded the [Japan Cartoonists Association Award](/wiki/Japan_Cartoonists_Association_Award "Japan Cartoonists Association Award") posthumously.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

In 2023, Sakaguchi's *Ishi no Hana* won the Prix du Patrimoine, which honors works that left their mark on history, at the 50th [Angoulême International Comics Festival](/wiki/Angoul%C3%AAme_International_Comics_Festival "Angoulême International Comics Festival").[[6]](#cite_note-6)
